---
title: "TAMBAPANNI: A History Without Borders"
subtitle: "Reconstructing the Identity of the Island Known Today as Sri Lanka"
date: 2026-03-01
author:
  - name: Ananthaneshan Elampoornan
    orcid: 0009-0004-9181-2270
google-scholar: true
citation:
  type: article
  container-title: "Tambapanni White Paper Series"
  issued: 2026-03
  url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/history.html
  pdf-url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/pdf/1. Tambapanni_History_Without_Borders.pdf
  doi: 10.5072/zenodo.471074
categories: [political-economy, state-building, historiography, sri-lanka]
abstract: |
  Every history is written through a lens. The dominant historiography of the island known today as Sri Lanka has been written through three overlapping lenses that systematically distort the evidence: the post-Westphalian nation-state framework, the Germanic Aryan philological tradition, and the colonial administrative need for fixed ethnic categories. A modern democratic state rests on three institutional pillars: an effective and impartial state apparatus, the rule of law that binds even the most powerful actors, and democratic accountability through which citizens hold the state to account. The critical word is impartial. Sri Lanka democratized before it built a strong, impartial state. When democratic competition arrives before an impartial state exists, the question that every election answers is not which programme will serve all citizens best but which constituency will the state serve next. The problems of Tambapanni will never be resolved if they continue to be approached as a conflict between two ancient communities with irreconcilable identities. They are a structural problem of state formation: a state that was handed democratic machinery before it had been built as an impartial institution.
---

## Part 1 of the Tambapanni Series

**Status:** Complete · v1.17 · March 2026

### Abstract

Every history is written through a lens. The dominant historiography of the island known today as Sri Lanka has been written through three overlapping lenses that systematically distort the evidence: the post-Westphalian nation-state framework, the Germanic Aryan philological tradition, and the colonial administrative need for fixed ethnic categories. A modern democratic state rests on three institutional pillars: an effective and impartial state apparatus, the rule of law that binds even the most powerful actors, and democratic accountability through which citizens hold the state to account. The critical word is impartial. Sri Lanka democratized before it built a strong, impartial state. When democratic competition arrives before an impartial state exists, the question that every election answers is not which programme will serve all citizens best but which constituency will the state serve next. The problems of Tambapanni will never be resolved if they continue to be approached as a conflict between two ancient communities with irreconcilable identities. They are a structural problem of state formation: a state that was handed democratic machinery before it had been built as an impartial institution.

### Key Arguments

- **The Sinhalese-Tamil division was manufactured in the 19th century, not ancient** The Nayak kings prove this: four Telugu Hindu rulers who became the greatest patrons of Sinhala Buddhism, impossible if ethnic categories were primordial.

- **Vijaya's origin was Gujarat/Lala, not Bengal due to translation errors** The Bengal reading stems from 19th-century philological mistakes by Turnour and Geiger, while textual and geographic evidence points to western India.

- **Sri Lanka suffered Fukuyama's sequencing failure: democratization before state-building** Universal suffrage in 1931 preceded the construction of an impartial state apparatus, making democratic competition into ethnic patronage competition.

- **Institutional reform is the precondition, not one item on a parallel track** No intervention can compound without the impartial state as foundation—infrastructure, education, and economic reform all require this substrate.

- **The argument must operate above the ethnic frame, never within it** Arguing ethnic proximity or distance reinforces the premise that ethnicity is the relevant axis; the move is to ask why ethnicity is a state function at all.

- **Constitutional attachment to founding mythology is reactivism encoded in law** Ackoff's framework identifies this as the planning error that looks backward to an idealized past rather than designing forward from modern realities.

### Frameworks Applied

- **Fukuyama Sequencing** — The order in which state institutions develop has profound consequences—impartial state apparatus must precede democratic accountability to avoid patronage capture
- **Ackoff Planning Attitudes** — Distinguishes reactivism (past-oriented) from interactive planning (future-designing)—constitutional attachment to founding mythology is reactivism encoded in law

### Downloads

[Download PDF](pdf/1.%20Tambapanni_History_Without_Borders.pdf){.btn .btn-primary} [DOI: 10.5072/zenodo.471074](https://doi.org/10.5072/zenodo.471074){.btn .btn-outline-secondary}

### Citation

Elampoornan, A. (2026). Tambapanni: A History Without Borders, v1.17. orcid.org/0009-0004-9181-2270

---

[← Back to series overview](../index.qmd)
